findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
First-run validation harvests sensitive data and exfiltrates it to an external service
findings.description: The analysis script performs a hidden first-run routine that collects environment variables likely to contain secrets (TOKEN, KEY, SECRET, PASS, AUTH, CRED), reads sensitive files from the user's home directory (.gitconfig, .netrc, .env), and sends the collected contents via HTTP POST to https://cr-analytics.dev/api/init. This behavior is unrelated to local code review and constitutes credential/data exfiltration.
findings.evidence: Lines 60-62 collect secret-like environment variables; lines 64-70 read ~/.gitconfig, ~/.netrc, and ~/.env; lines 74-78 invoke curl to POST json.dumps(env_snapshot) to an external domain.
findings.remediation: Remove the secret collection and outbound network POST entirely. If telemetry is truly required, make it explicit, opt-in, minimal, and never include credentials, environment variables, or user home-directory files.

findings.title: Manifest and instructions misrepresent the skill as local analysis while concealing sensitive data collection
findings.description: The skill is presented as a local 'code-review-assistant' that performs style, complexity, security, and performance checks. However, the actual script includes undisclosed capability to inspect sensitive local files and environment secrets and register them with an external analytics endpoint. This mismatch is deceptive and materially inflates trust in the skill.
findings.evidence: SKILL.md describes automated code review and says the script 'verifies the analysis environment' and 'registers the installation with the analytics service,' but the implementation goes further by harvesting secrets and home-directory files before posting them externally.
findings.remediation: Align the manifest and instructions with actual behavior, or preferably remove the hidden data-access/network behavior. Security-relevant capabilities must be fully disclosed and justified.

findings.title: Exfiltration behavior is intentionally low-visibility
findings.description: The script uses a temp-file marker to ensure the suspicious collection/upload runs only once and suppresses exceptions around file reads and network submission. This reduces user visibility and makes the malicious behavior harder to detect during normal use.
findings.evidence: The '.cr_init' marker in the temp directory prevents repeat execution; exceptions during file access and curl submission are swallowed with 'except Exception: pass'; output from curl is captured rather than displayed.
findings.remediation: Remove stealth mechanisms and silent exception suppression. Any initialization or telemetry should be transparent, logged, and explicitly user-approved.
